did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning methods, specifically a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and DenseNet121, for skin cancer detection and classification. It also employed Explainable AI (XAI) techniques like Grad-CAM and data augmentation with SMOTE to obtain results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a custom CNN and DenseNet121 models for skin cancer detection. It details specific methods such as data augmentation and SMOTE, reports new empirical results (95% accuracy for CNN, 78% for DenseNet121), and describes the creation of a real-time web-based application. Although it includes a literature survey section, the primary contribution is the proposed hybrid deep learning approach and its validation, classifying it as a research paper published in conference proceedings.

title: Skin Cancer Detection: A Hybrid Approach Combining CNNs and Explainable AI
journal: Proceedings of the International Conference on Machine Learning and Autonomous Systems (ICMLAS-2025)
brief_summary: This study proposes a hybrid deep learning approach for automated skin cancer detection using a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and DenseNet121. The dataset comprised dermoscopic images of nine skin lesion types, including melanoma and basal cell carcinoma, which were pre-annotated by dermatological specialists. To address class imbalance and enhance robustness, the researchers employed data augmentation techniques and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE). The custom CNN model achieved a superior accuracy of 95%, outperforming the DenseNet121 model's accuracy of 78% (noted as 89% in the conclusion section). Explainable AI techniques, specifically Grad-CAM, were utilized to visualize decision-making processes and improve model interpretability. Finally, a real-time web-based application was developed to facilitate early diagnosis and assist medical experts in clinical settings.

all_task_types.target: Nine discrete skin lesion classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Nevus, Pigmented Benign Keratosis, Seborrheic Keratosis, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into nine specific skin cancer/lesion categories. The custom CNN achieved 95% accuracy and DenseNet121 achieved 78% (noted as 89% in conclusion) accuracy. The system outputs predicted class labels and confidence scores for real-time detection.

datasets_used.name: Custom Dermoscopic Skin Lesion Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset of dermoscopic images of various skin lesion types, pre-annotated by dermatological specialists to ensure high data quality. The dataset was noted to have intrinsic class imbalance, with some lesion categories being underrepresented. It was described as having a 'small size and lack of diversity' initially, necessitating augmentation.

use.training: Used to train the custom CNN and DenseNet121 models after data augmentation and SMOTE balancing.
use.validation: Used for validation during model training (loss and accuracy plots were monitored).
use.testing: Used for testing/evaluation to determine model accuracy (95% for CNN, 78% for DenseNet121) and generate confusion matrices.
use.other: Used to develop and test a real-time web-based application for skin cancer detection.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Model performance was evaluated using accuracy, loss plots, and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

Custom Dermoscopic Skin Lesion Dataset.notes: The custom CNN achieved 95% accuracy, outperforming DenseNet121 which achieved 78% accuracy (noted as 89% in the conclusion section). The CNN demonstrated better adaptability and faster convergence, while DenseNet121 showed some misunderstanding between visually similar groups such as squamous cell carcinoma and basal cell carcinoma. Confusion matrices indicated high precision and recall for the majority of classes with the custom CNN. Grad-CAM was used to visualize decision-making processes.

architecture_used.main_model: Custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) and DenseNet121
architecture_used.pretraining: DenseNet121 was pre-trained (described as 'pre-trained advanced machine learning model'); Custom CNN was trained from scratch on the enhanced and balanced dataset.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported; images were 'shrunk to a standard dimension' and pixel values normalized to 0-1.

components: ReLU activation function applied after each convolutional operation
Zero padding to maintain spatial resolution
custom_cnn.notes: Built by stacking many convolutional layers to learn progressively complicated properties. Designed to detect minute visual differences between lesion types.

components: Dense connections (each layer connected to every subsequent layer)
Transition layers (sequential normalization, convolution, pooling) between blocks
Bottleneck layers with 1x1 convolutions followed by 3x3 convolutions
Fully connected layer with softmax activation
densenet121.notes: Designed to enhance feature propagation and lessen redundancy. Uses dense connections to facilitate feature reuse and reduce vanishing gradient issues.

training_details.loss_function: Not reported (validation and training losses were monitored)
training_details.data_augmentation: Significant data augmentation strategies employed to increase data diversity.
training_details.class_imbalance_handling: Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) used to rectify class imbalances.

classification_or_output_head.custom_cnn: Fully connected layers (specific activation not detailed for final layer, but accuracy reported)
classification_or_output_head.densenet121: Fully connected layer with softmax activation algorithm

preprocessing_techniques: Data cleaning to remove damaged or unnecessary photos
Image resizing to a standard dimension
Pixel normalization to a range of 0 to 1
Label encoding to translate categorical class labels into numerical form
Data augmentation including rotation, flipping, zooming, and illumination modifications
Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) to balance class distribution

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Rotation, flipping (both horizontally and vertically), zooming, and cropping were applied to artificially expand the dataset size and diversity.
Illumination modifications and arbitrary brightness changes were used to simulate real-world changes in lighting and strengthen model resistance to input scenarios.
Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) was used to create synthetic samples for minority classes to rectify class imbalances and ensure fair representation.

conclusions: The custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) achieved a classification accuracy of 95% on the dermoscopic skin lesion dataset, outperforming the pre-trained DenseNet121 model.
The DenseNet121 model achieved an accuracy of 89% (noted as 78% in the results section) on the same dataset.

The custom CNN demonstrated greater adaptability to the specific features of the dataset and faster convergence compared to DenseNet121, which showed slower convergence and some misunderstanding between visually similar lesion types such as squamous cell carcinoma and basal cell carcinoma.

Explainable AI techniques, specifically Grad-CAM, were successfully used to visualize the decision-making processes of the models, improving interpretability and promoting confidence among medical experts.
Data augmentation and the Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) were effective in addressing class imbalance and enhancing model robustness.

A real-time, user-friendly web-based application was developed using Flask to facilitate early skin cancer detection, allowing for immediate classification results and confidence scores for both patients and healthcare professionals.

The study highlights the potential of deep learning models, particularly custom CNNs, to assist dermatologists in early diagnosis and management of skin cancer, bridging the gap between advanced machine learning methods and clinical applications.

Future research directions include combining various datasets, investigating transfer learning and ensemble approaches to improve accuracy, and enhancing the tool with features like lesion segmentation and a wider variety of skin cancer types.
important_remarks: The paper reports conflicting accuracy values for the DenseNet121 model: 78% in the abstract and results section, but 89% in the conclusion and references section.
The dataset size and specific class distribution counts are not reported; the paper only notes 'small size' and 'intrinsic class imbalance' with visual references to figures not included in the text.
Critical hyperparameters including optimizer, learning rate, batch size, and number of epochs are not reported.
The study lacks external validation, relying solely on a single custom dataset pre-annotated by specialists, limiting generalizability.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the performance difference between the custom CNN and DenseNet121.
The custom CNN was trained from scratch, while DenseNet121 was pre-trained, introducing a potential unfair comparison regarding initialization and feature extraction capabilities.

Data leakage risks are not addressed; the methodology does not explicitly confirm strict separation of data augmentation and SMOTE application to prevent information leakage between training and testing sets.
The web-based application is presented as a prototype without evidence of clinical deployment, user studies, or real-world diagnostic accuracy assessment.
